title: Color-avoiding Paths
speaker: Yuval Wigderson
abstract:
The very first result ever proved about tournaments is due to Rédei, who nearly 100 years ago proved that every tournament contains a Hamiltonian directed path. Since then, questions and results about directed paths in tournaments have become a major topic in extremal combinatorics and Ramsey theory.
In this talk, I will discuss some new results on color-avoiding directed paths, that is, directed paths in an edge-colored tournament whose edges all avoid some fixed color. Somewhat surprisingly, such questions turn out to have deep connections to geometric packing, error-correcting codes, k-majority tournaments, Ramsey theory for sequences, extremal problems in convex geometry, Hilbert's inequality, and hypergraph Turán questions, and I will do my best to present (some of) these connections. 
Based on joint work with Jacob Fox and Benny Sudakov.

proof_stages.label: Hamiltonian paths in tournaments by local swaps
proof_stages.summary: The talk begins with Rédei’s theorem that every tournament has a Hamiltonian directed path, proved algorithmically by repeatedly swapping adjacent vertices joined by a backward edge and using the increasing number of forward edges as a terminating measure.

proof_stages.label: Ordered two-color paths and the Erdős–Szekeres labeling proof
proof_stages.summary: The Erdős–Szekeres monotone-subsequence theorem is reformulated as the existence of a long monotone monochromatic path in a red/blue ordered complete graph, and Seidenberg’s proof labels each vertex by the longest blue and red monotone paths ending there.

proof_stages.label: Chromatic-number path theorem via a maximal acyclic subgraph
proof_stages.summary: The Gallai–Hasse–Roy–Vitaver theorem is proved by replacing a false longest-path labeling on the whole orientation with the same labeling on a maximal acyclic subgraph, yielding a proper coloring of the original graph and hence a directed path of length at least the chromatic number.

proof_stages.label: Monochromatic paths in q-colored tournaments
proof_stages.summary: Using submultiplicativity of chromatic number under graph union, one color class in any q-edge-coloring of an n-vertex tournament has chromatic number at least n^{1/q}, so the previous theorem gives a monochromatic directed path of that length; this subsumes the earlier warmups and is sharp already for transitive tournaments.

proof_stages.label: Definition and motivations for color-avoiding paths
proof_stages.summary: The main parameter g_q(n) is defined as the worst-case longest directed path in a q-colored n-vertex tournament that avoids at least one color, and the speaker motivates it through Ramsey theory, tripod packing, convex-geometric hypergraph problems, majority tournaments, and probabilistic/Fourier-analytic connections.

proof_stages.label: Known bounds and the high-color lower theorem
proof_stages.summary: After recalling the transitive upper bound g_q(n)≤n^{1-1/q}, the trivial √n lower bound, and better three-color upper constructions, the speaker states the new theorem g_q(n)≥n^{1-O(1/√log q)} and later sketches that its remaining proof uses an induction on two halves with an amortized balancing over colors.

proof_stages.label: Far-from-transitive sparse-color path lemma
proof_stages.summary: A key lemma says that a tournament δ-far from transitive contains a linear-length directed path using at most three colors, so for q≥4 the far-from-transitive case immediately gives a color-avoiding path and the rest of the theorem can focus on tournaments close to transitive; the Q&A clarifies the required near-complete tournament structure and δ-dependence.

proof_stages.label: Cyclic-triangle proof of the far-from-transitive lemma
proof_stages.summary: The lemma is proved by a robust tournament triangle-removal fact giving many cyclic triangles, pigeonholing to a single three-color pattern, pruning to edges lying in many such triangles, and then walking through successive triangles to build a long directed path using only those three colors.

examples.name: Bubble-sort interpretation for a transitive tournament
examples.note: The speaker compares the swap algorithm for a Hamiltonian path to bubble sort, and in the transitive-tournament special case the algorithm literally sorts the unique Hamiltonian order; this clarifies the algorithmic proof rather than generating it.
examples.engagement_reason: he traces the adjacent-swap Hamiltonian-path algorithm and its forward-edge progress invariant, then identifies the transitive-tournament case with literal bubble sort toward the unique order
